mnemonics.title: TRS - Translate Rotate Scale
mnemonics.content: The three basic 2D transformations: Translation (sliding), Rotation (spinning), and Scaling (resizing). Remember: 'Taj Rani Sax' - The Taj Mahal was RotateD and Scaled for the king.

mnemonics.title: Rotation Matrix Memory
mnemonics.content: COS -SIN (first row), SIN +COS (second row). Remember: 'Coffee Sin, Tea Cos' - First drink COFFEE (COS-SIN), then drink TEA (SIN+COS).

mnemonics.title: Shear Matrix Elements
mnemonics.content: Shx (shear-x) modifies x using y: [1 shx 0; 0 1 0]. Shy (shear-y) modifies y using x: [1 0 0; shy 1 0]. Remember: 'X gets help from Y' and 'Y gets help from X'.

mnemonics.title: Homogeneous Coordinates
mnemonics.content: Add a third coordinate '1' to all 2D points: (x, y) → [x y 1]. This allows matrix multiplication for translation. Remember: 'Homeogeneous = Home (1) at the end'.

mnemonics.title: Reflection Types
mnemonics.content: X-axis reflection: y becomes -y. Y-axis reflection: x becomes -x. Remember: 'Xerox copies flip X' - Flip across X-axis changes Y values.

mnemonics.title: Order Matters in Composite Transformations
mnemonics.content: Matrix multiplication is not commutative: A×B ≠ B×A. Remember: 'Put On Shoes, Not Shoes Put On' - Translation then Rotation ≠ Rotation then Translation.

acronyms.usage: The three fundamental 2D transformations that form the basis of all geometric operations in computer graphics.
Adding a third coordinate (w=1) to 2D points to enable matrix multiplication for all transformations including translation.

acronyms.expansion: Standard Transformation Matrix (for rotation, scaling, shear, reflection)
acronyms.usage: Used when origin is fixed; cannot represent translation. Format: [a b; c d] where ad-bc ≠ 0 for valid transformation.
Full transformation matrix including translation: [a b tx; c d ty; 0 0 1]. The last row [0 0 1] is always constant.
Standard order for building composite transformations in computer graphics: scale first, then rotate, then translate.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine sliding a photograph on a table - the object moves but its orientation and size remain unchanged. Think of a slider moving along x and y axes.
Imagine a clock hand rotating around the center point (origin). Positive angle = counter-clockwise (CCW). Picture yourself spinning a merry-go-round.
Think of zooming in (uniform scale >1) or out (uniform scale <1) on Google Maps. Non-uniform scaling is like stretching an image width-wise or height-wise.
Imagine parallel lines becoming slanted - like pushing the top of a rectangle to make it a parallelogram. Think of a deck of cards being pushed sideways.
Picture looking into a mirror - the image is flipped. X-axis reflection is a vertical mirror, Y-axis reflection is a horizontal mirror.
Think of a robot arm: first rotate the shoulder, then the elbow, then move the hand. Each transformation builds on the previous one in a chain.

analogies.analogy: Moving a chess piece on a board - it slides from one square to another without rotating or changing size. The piece maintains its orientation throughout.
Spinning a top or a steering wheel - the object rotates around a fixed center point. Like a fan blade turning around its hub.
Using a photocopier to enlarge or reduce an image. Think of a person growing (scale >1) or shrinking (scale <1) - all proportions change uniformly or non-uniformly.
A stack of papers being pushed from the side - the top sheets slide relative to the bottom. Also like a斜塔 (leaning tower) - it tilts but base stays fixed.
Looking at your reflection in a mirror or seeing your face upside down when looking at the front camera - the coordinates flip across the axis.
Putting on socks then shoes vs shoes then socks - both are different operations but order changes the final result. In transformations: Translate then Rotate ≠ Rotate then Translate.
Adding '1' is like giving every point a 'home address' in 3D space (z=1 plane). This allows all transformations to be treated uniformly as matrix multiplications.
